# ADR-35218: Typed Review-Lifecycle Outcome Evaluators With Persisted Manifest Metadata

**Date**: 2026-05-27
**Status**: Draft
**Deciders**: Unknown — generated by the Design Decision Gate from PR #35218 evidence

---

## Part 1 — Narrative (Human-Friendly)

### Context

Outcome evaluation runs after a safe-output workflow finishes and classifies each emitted item (issue, comment, review, …) as accepted / rejected / pending / unknown based on the current state of the target object on GitHub. Prior to this change the post-hoc evaluator for review-lifecycle items (`add_reviewer`, `submit_pull_request_review`) relied on the same existence-only fallback path that ADR-style "did the issue/PR survive?" evaluators used (see the new `target_exists_only` signal added alongside this work in [evaluate_outcomes.cjs](../../actions/setup/js/evaluate_outcomes.cjs)). Existence is meaningless for review-lifecycle outcomes: the question is whether the *requested reviewer actually reviewed*, whether the *submitted review was approved / dismissed / followed by a merge*, not whether the PR or review object exists. The signals needed to answer those questions — the requested reviewer/team identity, the review id and event — are only knowable at execution time inside the handler, but the manifest schema previously did not carry per-item execution-time metadata.

### Decision

We will introduce **dedicated, type-specific outcome evaluators** for `add_reviewer` and `submit_pull_request_review`, implemented symmetrically in Go ([`pkg/cli/outcome_eval_review.go`](../../pkg/cli/outcome_eval_review.go)) and JavaScript ([`actions/setup/js/evaluate_outcomes.cjs`](../../actions/setup/js/evaluate_outcomes.cjs)), and we will extend the safe-output manifest schema with an optional **per-item `metadata` map** that handlers populate at execution time so evaluators can correlate post-hoc GitHub state back to the requested action. The evaluators classify against a defined matrix of review-lifecycle states (approved, submitted, request removed, dismissed, changes-requested-then-merged, closed-after-review, latest-review-pending, awaiting-review) with explicit `outcome_status` / `evidence_strength` / `signal` tuples instead of being collapsed into generic accepted/rejected buckets. To support the deferred review-submission flow, handlers that only buffer pre-submit comments now mark their result with `deferred_manifest: true` (and `add_reviewer` uses `skipped: true` when there is nothing to log), so the manifest logger excludes those non-final results.

### Alternatives Considered

#### Alternative 1: Keep existence-only fallback evaluation

We could have continued classifying review-lifecycle items by checking only that the referenced PR/review object still exists, mapping that to `unknown / weak / target_exists_only`. We rejected this because it would have left review-lifecycle outputs indistinguishable from each other — there is no business value in knowing "the review still exists" when the question the audit pipeline actually needs to answer is "did the reviewer approve / dismiss / merge after changes". The whole point of the dedicated evaluators is to surface that distinction.

#### Alternative 2: Recompute correlation from URL parsing + post-hoc heuristics, without persisted metadata

We could have skipped extending the manifest schema and instead inferred the relevant correlation keys (which reviewer was requested, which review id was submitted) by replaying the run logs or parsing the review URL fragment (`#pullrequestreview-101`). We rejected this because URL parsing only recovers a single id (the review id is in the fragment for `submit_pull_request_review`, but `add_reviewer` does not produce a URL at all — the change explicitly handles the no-URL case in `evaluateItem` and routes by type), and log replay would couple the audit pipeline to log formats that are not part of the safe-output contract. Persisting structured metadata at execution time is both simpler and more durable.

#### Alternative 3: Single shared evaluator with branching on item type

We could have added the new lifecycle logic inline in the existing `evaluateItem` switch in `evaluate_outcomes.cjs` (and similarly grown `outcome_eval.go`) rather than extracting `evaluateAddReviewer` / `evaluateSubmitPullRequestReview` into their own functions and the Go evaluator into its own file. We rejected this because the review-lifecycle classification matrix is substantially larger than the existing per-type evaluators (eight branches each, with API fan-out to `/reviews`, `/requested_reviewers`, `/commits`), and inlining would have obscured the dispatch logic in `evaluateItem` while making the new evaluators harder to test in isolation. The dedicated functions are independently exported (`module.exports` adds `evaluateAddReviewer` and `evaluateSubmitPullRequestReview`) and the Go side gets its own `outcome_eval_review.go` file.

### Consequences

#### Positive
- Review-lifecycle outputs now carry actionable signals (`review_approved`, `review_dismissed`, `changes_requested_addressed`, `latest_review_pending`, etc.) instead of collapsing into generic accepted/pending buckets.
- The persisted `metadata` map on manifest items establishes a reusable channel for any future evaluator that needs execution-time context (e.g. a future `add_labels` evaluator could persist the labels it actually applied).
- Evidence strength is explicit per branch (strong / medium / weak), giving downstream audit metrics a defensible confidence axis.
- `deferred_manifest` and `skipped` flags prevent pre-submit buffer entries and no-op reviewer requests from polluting the manifest as if they were completed outcomes.
- Go and JS implementations are kept aligned by mirroring the same classification matrix and the same `normalizeOutcome` detail strings.

#### Negative
- The classification matrix and metadata-extraction helpers are duplicated across Go (`outcome_eval_review.go`, ~412 lines) and JS (`evaluate_outcomes.cjs`, ~326 added lines). Any future change to a branch must be made twice and tested twice; drift is a real risk over time.
- The manifest schema grows a new optional field (`metadata`) that must be carried through `CreatedItemReport` in `pkg/cli/audit_report.go`, every manifest log site, and the `extractCreatedItemFromResult` normalization path. Older manifest files predate this field and will produce `unknown` outcomes when re-evaluated.
- The classification depends on a narrow time-window heuristic (`isOnOrAfter` / `timestampOnOrAfter`) to scope reviews/commits relative to the safe-output timestamp; malformed or missing candidate timestamps are conservatively excluded, which can under-attribute some real activity.
- `evaluateItem` now has two new early dispatch paths (one for items without a URL, one for items with a URL) that duplicate the per-type routing — the dispatch table grew rather than getting consolidated.

#### Neutral
- `evaluateItem` accepts either a `(endpoint) => any` function or the prior `{ ghAPI, nowMs }` options object via a polymorphic `apiOrOptions` parameter; this preserves the existing test seam shape while enabling the simpler new test-API form used in the new tests.
- Manifest entries continue to be append-only JSONL; the schema change is additive and old readers will simply ignore the new field.
- The reviewer set passed to the manifest distinguishes `requested_reviewers` from `requested_team_reviewers`, but classification only requires presence/absence — additional team-resolution logic is intentionally out of scope.

---

## Part 2 — Normative Specification (RFC 2119)

> The key words **MUST**, **MUST NOT**, **REQUIRED**, **SHALL**, **SHALL NOT**, **SHOULD**, **SHOULD NOT**, **RECOMMENDED**, **MAY**, and **OPTIONAL** in this section are to be interpreted as described in [RFC 2119](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2119).

### Safe-Output Manifest Schema

1. Manifest entries for `add_reviewer` **MUST** carry a `metadata` object containing `requested_reviewers` (array of GitHub login strings) and `requested_team_reviewers` (array of team slug strings) when at least one reviewer or team was requested.
2. Manifest entries for `submit_pull_request_review` **MUST** carry a `metadata` object containing the submitted `review_id` (integer) and `review_event` (string) captured from the final review submission.
3. The manifest logger **MUST NOT** record an entry whose handler result has `staged === true`, `skipped === true`, or `deferred_manifest === true`.
4. The `CreatedItemReport` Go struct **MUST** expose `metadata` as `map[string]any` with the JSON tag `metadata,omitempty`.
5. The manifest schema **MAY** be extended with additional metadata keys per item type; readers **MUST** tolerate unknown keys.

### Review-Lifecycle Evaluators

1. The outcome evaluator dispatch table **MUST** route `add_reviewer` items to `evalAddReviewer` / `evaluateAddReviewer` and `submit_pull_request_review` items to `evalSubmitPullRequestReview` / `evaluateSubmitPullRequestReview` before falling back to URL-only or existence-only logic.
2. Each review-lifecycle evaluator **MUST** emit an `OutcomeEvaluation` (or equivalent JS object) with all three of: `outcome_status` ∈ {`accepted`, `rejected`, `pending`, `unknown`}, `evidence_strength` ∈ {`strong`, `medium`, `weak`}, and a non-empty `signal` string drawn from the controlled vocabulary defined in `normalizeOutcome`.
3. `evalAddReviewer` **MUST** classify as `accepted / strong / review_approved` when a requested reviewer submitted an `APPROVED` review on or after the item timestamp.
4. `evalAddReviewer` **MUST** classify as `accepted / medium / review_submitted` when a requested reviewer submitted any non-pending review on or after the item timestamp, or when a team review request received any non-pending review after the item timestamp.
5. `evalAddReviewer` **MUST** classify as `rejected / strong / review_request_removed` when the requested reviewers and teams have all been removed from the pending set and no review was submitted by them.
6. `evalAddReviewer` **MUST** classify as `pending / medium / awaiting_review` when the requested reviewers or teams remain in the PR's pending-reviewers list.
7. `evalSubmitPullRequestReview` **MUST** classify as `rejected / strong / review_dismissed` when the persisted review's state is `DISMISSED`.
8. `evalSubmitPullRequestReview` **MUST** classify as `accepted / strong / review_approved` when the PR is merged and the persisted review state is `APPROVED`.
9. `evalSubmitPullRequestReview` **MUST** classify as `accepted / medium / changes_requested_addressed` when the PR is merged, the persisted review state is `CHANGES_REQUESTED`, and at least one commit was pushed after the review submission.
10. `evalSubmitPullRequestReview` **MUST** classify as `rejected / medium / closed_without_merge_after_review` when the PR is closed and not merged.
11. `evalSubmitPullRequestReview` **MUST** classify as `pending / medium / latest_review_pending` when the PR is open and the persisted review is the most recent review on the PR.
12. When a review-lifecycle evaluator cannot correlate the persisted item with current GitHub state (missing PR/repo, API error, or no matching review), it **MUST** return an `unknown` outcome with a weak evidence strength rather than guessing.
13. Implementations **MUST NOT** rely on URL parsing alone for review-lifecycle correlation; the persisted `metadata` fields are the canonical correlation source.

### Cross-Language Parity

1. The JavaScript collector in `evaluate_outcomes.cjs` and the Go evaluator in `pkg/cli/outcome_eval_review.go` **MUST** classify the same input state into the same `outcome_status` and `evidence_strength` tuple.
2. The `signal` vocabulary used by both implementations **SHOULD** match, and this implementation uses a shared signal set (for example, both Go and JS emit `awaiting_review` for pending reviewer requests and `review_approved` for approved+merged reviews).
3. Any new lifecycle branch added to one implementation **MUST** be added to the other in the same change set, and **MUST** be covered by tests in both languages.

### Conformance

An implementation is considered conformant with this ADR if it satisfies all **MUST** and **MUST NOT** requirements above. The existing tests in `actions/setup/js/evaluate_outcomes.test.cjs`, `actions/setup/js/safe_output_manifest.test.cjs`, and `pkg/cli/outcome_eval_test.go` are the executable conformance check for the corresponding sections. Failure to update both Go and JS sides for a lifecycle change, or to record `metadata` at execution time for the supported item types, constitutes non-conformance.

---
